design.gap: GAP B — Cross-row atomicity: complete the CAS rung (update_entry_if exists but has two documented sharp edges) and add transact([ops]) — a declarative, all-or-nothing multi-op batch (create/update/delete/update_if) executed in one Postgres transaction, with cross-op references, after-commit-only event emission, and batch-level idempotency so composite writes are safely retryable.
design.approach:
The CAS rung already ships (lib/entries.ts:250-326, MCP tool at lib/mcp/tools.ts:256/810), so increment 1 is not "build update_entry_if" but "finish it": (a) a 0-row CAS today collapses precondition-failure, increment-bounds-violation, and increment-field-absent into one opaque `conflict`, and (b) the CAS emit at lib/entries.ts:316 carries no pre-image, so `previous`/`changedFields` are missing from its event payload. B1 fixes both. The pre-image is an advisory SELECT before the UPDATE, used only for the event payload. The failure diagnosis is deliberately NOT a JS mirror of the WHERE clause: matchClause (lib/query.ts:145-173) coerces missing keys (String(undefined ?? "")==="", Boolean(undefined)===false) where SQL `data->>'f'` yields NULL and never matches, so a JS re-check would confidently misdiagnose. Instead, on a 0-row UPDATE, one diagnostic SELECT projects EVERY guard from the failed WHERE as a boolean column — each if-clause via the same compileClause fragments buildWhere used, the `data ? field` existence guard (lib/entries.ts:293), and each declared min/max arithmetic guard — plus the current data. Postgres evaluates them with byte-identical NULL semantics, so the verdicts cannot diverge from what the UPDATE saw at read time. Decision precedence over the returned booleans: row missing → not_found; any if-clause not true → 'conflict' naming the first failing clause with the row's actual value; increment field absent → 'unset' ("field \"seats\" is not set on this entry — set it with update_entry first"); a bounds guard not true → 'bounds' with the real current value; everything true → 'conflict' with "row changed concurrently between the update and this diagnosis — re-read and retry". The message lists ALL failed guards (they arrive in one row) and is hedged "as of the latest read", because on the single-op neon-http path the SELECT is a separate statement; inside transact (B4) the same helper runs on the still-open tx executor (a 0-row UPDATE is not a pg error), which eliminates that race entirely.

transact([ops]) has one hard infrastructure fact underneath it: the app's only DB client is drizzle-orm/neon-http (db/index.ts:1-7), which cannot run interactive transactions — and a non-interactive statement batch cannot inspect row counts mid-flight, so a missed CAS or delete could not abort the batch. The design therefore adds a second, transaction-capable client used ONLY by transact: a new lib/db-tx.ts exporting withTransaction(fn), built on @neondatabase/serverless's WebSocket Pool (already a dependency) + drizzle-orm/neon-serverless, pool created per call with max:1 and ended in finally — Neon's documented serverless pattern, identical on Netlify and Render (host-agnostic). Everything else keeps neon-http untouched. As a bonus this hands the platform an interactive-tx primitive (FOR UPDATE SKIP LOCKED becomes possible) that a future pg-backed job queue can reuse. transact itself is a thin composition of what exists: lib/entries.ts grows internal `*Core(dbc, ...)` variants of createEntry/updateEntry/deleteEntry/updateEntryIf that take a drizzle executor, do the exact same buildEntrySchema-strict-parse + verifyRefs + rethrowUnique work, and RETURN an emission descriptor instead of calling defer(emitEntryEvent) themselves; the public single-op functions become one-line wrappers (zero behavior change). transact validates every op up front, runs ops sequentially inside withTransaction; any validation error, unique violation, 0-row update, 0-row CAS, or 0-row delete throws TxAbort{opIndex, code, message} → full rollback. Delete semantics inside transact are stricter than delete_entry by design: a delete op on a missing entry aborts the batch with E_NOT_FOUND (an asserted precondition failed), and the tool description states this divergence out loud. Emission descriptors and audit rows are collected during the tx and handed to defer() only AFTER commit.

Composite writes must not lose the at-most-once protection single-op create_entry already has (idempotencyKey, lib/entries.ts:138-179): a lost HTTP response after commit would otherwise make the natural retry decrement a seat twice and duplicate a booking. B5 adds a BATCH-level optional idempotencyKey backed by a small `transact_receipts` ledger table (projectId, idempotencyKey with a unique index, results JSONB, createdAt). Because every create op's id is pre-generated client-side (crypto.randomUUID(), done in B2 from day one) and update/delete/update_if ids are inputs, the complete per-op result identity {op, collection, id} is known BEFORE execution — so the receipt is INSERTed as the FIRST statement inside the transaction, results included, no trailing UPDATE and no savepoints. A 23505 on that insert means the batch already committed: catch it (nothing else has executed, so aborting the tx is free), roll back, SELECT the stored receipt over neon-http, and return the stored results marked replayed:true — mirroring create_entry's conflict-then-select pattern. Replay remains distinguishable from entry unique-field violations by constraint name (receipt index vs entries_uq_*/entries_idempotency_*), preserving the documented data-core invariant for batches. Until B5 ships, B2's tool description states the retry hazard in plain words ("a timeout after commit is indistinguishable from failure; do not blind-retry — re-query state first"), per rule 1.

Cross-op references (B3) ride the ids B2 already pre-generates: a create op may declare `ref: "order"`; later ops reference it as the sentinel string "$ref:order" in relation-field values or as an op's `id`. Resolution happens BEFORE validation — the validator and verifyRefs see a real uuid — with static checks: refs may only point to EARLIER create ops, and a relation field using $ref:x must target the collection op x creates. verifyRefs gains an assumeExisting set so in-tx ids skip the DB existence probe. Error reporting throughout reuses the existing vocabulary: the tool returns the FAILING op's own ErrorCode (E_VALIDATION / E_NOT_FOUND / E_CONFLICT) with the message prefixed `op[2] update_if "seats": ...; transaction rolled back — no ops applied`. A dryRun flag (B4) returns a Terraform-style per-op validation report without opening a transaction.

keyDecisions.decision: Add a second, transaction-only DB client (lib/db-tx.ts — per-call @neondatabase/serverless WebSocket Pool max:1 + drizzle-orm/neon-serverless, pool.end() in finally); keep neon-http for every other query.
keyDecisions.rationale: neon-http (db/index.ts) cannot run interactive transactions, and transact NEEDS interactivity — a 0-row CAS or delete must be observed mid-transaction to abort. A per-call pool is Neon's documented serverless pattern, works identically on Netlify and Render, and gives future gaps (pg job queue with FOR UPDATE SKIP LOCKED) a reusable primitive.
keyDecisions.alternativeRejected: (a) Migrating the whole app to the WS driver — large blast radius and connection-lifecycle risk on every route for one feature. (b) neon-http's non-interactive transaction([stmts]) batch — cannot read row counts between statements, so aborting on a failed CAS would require SQL cleverness rule 5 forbids.

keyDecisions.decision: 0-row-CAS diagnosis is SQL-faithful: one diagnostic SELECT projects every guard of the failed WHERE (if-clauses via the same compileClause fragments, the `data ? field` existence guard, each declared min/max arithmetic guard) as boolean columns plus current data, evaluated by Postgres; JS only applies precedence over the returned verdicts. The originally-designed matchesClauses JS re-check is rejected outright.
keyDecisions.rationale: matchClause (lib/query.ts:145-173) coerces missing keys (String(undefined ?? "")==="", Boolean(undefined)===false) where SQL yields NULL-never-matches — a JS mirror would emit confidently WRONG fix hints (e.g. labeling a genuine if-clause failure on an absent boolean as 'bounds'), violating rule 2. Reusing the exact compiled fragments makes divergence structurally impossible, covers the existence guard the JS plan never evaluated, and one statement returns ALL verdicts so the message can list every failed guard. Explicit precedence closes every branch: not_found → failing if-clause ('conflict') → increment field absent ('unset', hint 'set it with update_entry first') → bounds arithmetic ('bounds', with real current value) → all-pass fallback ('conflict', 'row changed concurrently — re-read and retry'), so the no-min/max case and the race window both have honest answers. Messages are hedged 'as of the latest read' on the single-op path; inside transact the SELECT runs on the open tx executor, race-free. Reviewer issues 1 and 4 proposed slightly different precedence (conflict-first vs unset-first); since one SELECT yields all verdicts, the message reports every failed guard and the primary reason follows conflict-first — a failed stated precondition supersedes increment diagnostics, and nothing is hidden either way.
keyDecisions.alternativeRejected: (a) JS-side matchesClauses re-check (the original design) — three verified holes: NULL-semantics divergence on missing keys, never re-evaluates the data?field existence guard, and no valid branch when the field has no min/max. (b) Per-clause sequential SELECTs — N round-trips on neon-http and each read can see a different row version, making the combined verdict incoherent.

keyDecisions.decision: Refactor entry mutators into *Core(dbc, ...) functions that return an emission descriptor; transact collects descriptors during the tx and defer()s emitEntryEvent + recordAudit only after commit.
keyDecisions.rationale: lib/events.ts's contract is that lib/entries.ts is the ONLY emitter; cores preserve that single emit point while making 'events fire after commit only' structural rather than disciplined — a rolled-back create can never send a webhook or email.
keyDecisions.alternativeRejected: Calling the existing createEntry/updateEntry inside the tx — they defer(emitEntryEvent) immediately, so a rollback after op 3 would still fire ops 1-2's webhooks; they also hard-bind the neon-http `db`.

keyDecisions.decision: Batch-level optional idempotencyKey on transact, backed by a transact_receipts ledger row INSERTed as the FIRST statement inside the transaction with the full per-op result ids (all knowable pre-execution because create ids are pre-generated in B2); 23505 on the receipt's unique index → rollback (nothing else ran) → select receipt on neon-http → return stored results as replayed:true.
keyDecisions.rationale: The flagship composite (CAS seat decrement + booking create) is exactly the flow that must survive a lost response after commit; without this, upgrading from single ops to transact would WEAKEN at-most-once guarantees. Insert-first means replay detection fails fast, needs no savepoints, works over the WS driver, and keeps the data-core invariant 'idempotency replay vs unique-violation are distinguishable' — discrimination is by constraint name (receipt index vs entries_uq_*), same technique rethrowUnique already uses. One new table via db:push, one extra INSERT per keyed batch. Until B5 ships, B2's description states the retry hazard out loud (rule 1).
keyDecisions.alternativeRejected: (a) The original design's 'no idempotency, description points at create_entry' — it only evaluated OP-level keys (savepoint-per-create replay, rightly rejected) and never considered batch-level keys; pointing at create_entry does nothing for multi-op composites. (b) Op-level idempotencyKey with savepoints — 'op 1 replayed, op 2 executed fresh' is a semantics trap and savepoint ergonomics over the WS driver are unproven here. (c) Storing full result data snapshots in the receipt — result ids suffice for the replay envelope; agents re-query for current data, and snapshots would bloat the ledger.

keyDecisions.decision: A delete op inside transact on a missing entry ABORTS the batch with E_NOT_FOUND — deliberately stricter than delete_entry's silent no-op (lib/entries.ts:328-348, tools.ts:839-844) — and the tool description states the divergence: 'unlike delete_entry, a delete op on a missing entry aborts the whole batch with E_NOT_FOUND'.
keyDecisions.rationale: In an all-or-nothing batch, a delete the caller asserted should happen but can't is a failed precondition; silently skipping it would make 'applied' ambiguous. The original design contradicted itself (B2 said abort, invariantCompliance claimed exact delete_entry parity) — that parity claim is removed; per rule 1 the boundary lives in the tool description.
keyDecisions.alternativeRejected: No-op parity with delete_entry — silently 'succeeding' at a delete that didn't happen inside a batch whose whole value proposition is precondition enforcement; also would make deleteEntryCore's 0-row result meaningless to the abort logic.

keyDecisions.decision: transact failures surface the FAILING op's existing ErrorCode (E_VALIDATION/E_NOT_FOUND/E_CONFLICT) with an 'op[i] <op> "<collection>"' message prefix and an explicit 'transaction rolled back — no ops applied' suffix; no new top-level error code.
keyDecisions.rationale: ERROR_CODES is append-only forever (lib/error-codes.ts:4-5), and agents already branch on E_CONFLICT-retry vs E_VALIDATION-repair; keeping the underlying code preserves that signal while the message pinpoints the op. B1's reason taxonomy maps: not_found→E_NOT_FOUND; conflict/unset/bounds→E_CONFLICT with guard-specific messages.
keyDecisions.alternativeRejected: A blanket new E_TXN_FAILED code wrapping a JSON detail blob — erases code-branching, forces message parsing, and a wrong code choice is permanent under the append-only rule.

keyDecisions.decision: Cross-op refs = client-generated UUIDs for creates (pre-generated in B2 for the receipt ledger anyway) + named `ref`/'$ref:name' sentinel, resolved to real uuids BEFORE validation; verifyRefs gains an assumeExisting set for in-tx ids.
keyDecisions.rationale: entries.id is defaultRandom with no server dependency, so pre-generating ids lets buildEntrySchema and verifyRefs run unmodified on the final stored values — strict validation sees exactly what will be written. Named refs allow a static check that a relation $ref targets the collection the referenced op creates; the sentinel is interpreted only in relation-typed fields and op.id positions, and no uuid starts with '$'.
keyDecisions.alternativeRejected: (a) Positional $ops.0.id — breaks silently when an agent reorders ops. (b) Resolving refs server-side after each insert — forces validation to run on placeholders, weakening the one-definition-of-valid invariant.

keyDecisions.decision: B1 pre-image via an advisory SELECT before the CAS UPDATE, used ONLY for the success-path event's previous/changedFields; the CAS itself stays one statement, and the failure diagnosis is the separate SQL-faithful SELECT (previous decision).
keyDecisions.rationale: Conditions remain atomically inside the UPDATE's WHERE (the actual correctness invariant, lib/entries.ts:265-297); the pre-image only feeds the event payload, where 'one version behind under extreme contention' is acceptable and documented in the payload semantics.
keyDecisions.alternativeRejected: UPDATE ... FROM (SELECT ...) prev self-join RETURNING prev.data — under READ COMMITTED EvalPlanQual the joined prev row can mismatch the re-checked target row, a subtle wrong-pre-image bug no smoke test would catch; RETURNING OLD needs pg18.

increments.name: CAS completion: SQL-faithful failure diagnosis + pre-image on events
increments.what: (1) updateEntryIf (lib/entries.ts:250-326) gains an advisory pre-read of the row (same shape as updateEntry's fetch at :193-201) so the 'updated' emit at :316 passes `previous` — giving CAS events previous/changedFields parity with plain updates. (2) New diagnoseCasFailure(dbc, collection, id, opts) helper: on a 0-row UPDATE, run ONE diagnostic SELECT on the (id, collectionId) row projecting boolean columns for every guard the failed WHERE contained — each if-clause compiled by the SAME compileClause fragments buildWhere used (lib/query.ts needs a small export: per-item compiled fragments, e.g. buildWhereParts returning {label, sql}[] alongside buildWhere), the `data ? field` existence guard when increment is present, and one column per declared min/max arithmetic guard — plus the current data. Postgres evaluates with identical NULL semantics; JS treats any non-true as failed and applies precedence: no row → not_found; any if-clause failed → reason 'conflict' naming the first failing clause with the row's actual value (message lists ALL failed guards); increment field absent → reason 'unset' with message 'increment: field "seats" is not set on this entry — set it with update_entry first'; a bounds guard failed → reason 'bounds' with message 'increment seats by -1 would go below min 0 — current value is 0'; all guards pass → reason 'conflict' with 'row changed concurrently between the update and this diagnosis — re-read and retry'. Every message hedged 'as of the latest read'. UpdateIfResult becomes { ok:false; reason:'not_found'|'conflict'|'unset'|'bounds'; failedGuards?: string[]; current?: Record<string,unknown> }. Explicitly do NOT use matchesClauses for diagnosis (its missing-key coercion diverges from SQL). (3) MCP handler (lib/mcp/tools.ts:810-838) keeps E_NOT_FOUND for not_found and E_CONFLICT for conflict/unset/bounds (code stability) with the guard-specific message; tool description (:256-264) names all four failure modes. (4) Extend scripts/smoke/14-update-if.test.mjs: absent-field increment → 'unset' message; boolean eq-false if-clause on a row missing the key → 'conflict' naming that clause (regression pin against JS-coercion misdiagnosis); increment failure on a field with NO min/max (guards = existence + if only); bounds message with current value; webhook receiver asserting the updated event now carries previous/changedFields.
increments.surface: Changed MCP tool: update_entry_if (guard-specific E_CONFLICT/E_NOT_FOUND messages, unchanged codes + args — backward compatible); its emitted entry.updated webhook/email payload now includes previous + changedFields.

increments.name: transact([ops]) core: all-or-nothing create/update/delete over an interactive tx
increments.what: (1) New lib/db-tx.ts: withTransaction<T>(fn: (tx: DbExecutor) => Promise<T>) — lazily builds a @neondatabase/serverless Pool({max:1}) with neonConfig.webSocketConstructor = ws, wraps it in drizzle-orm/neon-serverless with the shared schema, runs drizzle's transaction(fn), pool.end() in finally; export a structural DbExecutor type both drizzle flavors satisfy. Add `ws` to package.json (+ serverExternalPackages in next.config.ts if bundling complains). (2) lib/entries.ts: extract createEntryCore/updateEntryCore/deleteEntryCore(dbc, ...) — identical validate + verifyRefs + rethrowUnique logic, but return { entry, emit: {event, entry, previous?} } instead of defer()ing; existing single-op functions become wrappers over the cores with `db` + defer + recordAudit (zero behavior change; existing smoke suite is the regression net). (3) New transact(projectId, ops, actor): zod-validated ops array (1..25) of {op:'create',collection,data} | {op:'update',collection,id,data} | {op:'delete',collection,id}; every create op gets an explicit crypto.randomUUID() id up front (entries.id defaultRandom accepts explicit ids; needed by B3 refs and B5 receipts); resolve all collections, validate + verifyRefs every op BEFORE the tx; then withTransaction runs cores sequentially — any ValidationError, unique violation, 0-row update, or 0-row DELETE throws TxAbort{opIndex, code, message} → rollback. Delete divergence is deliberate and documented: unlike delete_entry's silent no-op, a delete op on a missing entry aborts the batch with E_NOT_FOUND. After commit: one defer() emitting all collected events in order + recordAudit per op. (4) MCP tool `transact` appended to TOOL_DEFS + callTool switch case: description states boundaries out loud — all-or-nothing; max 25 ops; entry ops only (no schema ops); delete-on-missing aborts (unlike delete_entry); events/audit fire only after commit; MCP-only (not on the delivery API); AND the retry hazard until B5 lands: 'a timeout after commit is indistinguishable from failure — do not blind-retry; re-query state first (batch idempotencyKey planned)'. ok payload {applied:true, results:[{op, collection, id}]}; failure = err(underlying code, 'op[i] ...; transaction rolled back — no ops applied'). New scripts/smoke/17-transact.test.mjs: happy path (create+update+delete across two collections), mid-batch validation failure leaves zero rows, delete-of-missing-id aborts with E_NOT_FOUND and op index, unique-violation rollback, webhook receiver asserts no events on rollback and ordered events on commit.
increments.surface: New MCP tool: transact. No delivery-API or admin-UI surface (MCP-only; description says so). New internal primitive withTransaction reusable platform-wide.

increments.name: Cross-op references: ref/$ref over the pre-generated ids
increments.what: Create ops may declare `ref: string` (/^[a-z][a-z0-9_]*$/, unique across the batch); B2 already pre-generates every create's uuid, so B3 is purely the naming + resolution layer. A later op may use '$ref:<name>' as (a) a relation-field value inside data, or (b) its own `id`. A resolution pass before validation substitutes real uuids and statically enforces: $ref must name a declared ref; the referenced op must precede the referencing op ('refs may only point to earlier create ops — reorder the ops'); a relation field using $ref:x must have targetCollection equal to op x's collection ('field "author" targets "users" but $ref:order creates in "orders"'). verifyRefs (lib/entries.ts:29-97) gains an optional assumeExisting: Set<string> — ids in the set skip the DB existence probe (guaranteed by insert order inside the tx); all other refs still probe as today. The sentinel is interpreted ONLY in relation-typed fields and op.id positions — a '$ref:' string in a text field is stored literally; the tool description states this boundary. Update transact TOOL_DEF inputSchema + description with a worked example (create order → create line_item with order: '$ref:order'). Extend 17-transact.test.mjs: create-then-relate in one batch, forward-ref rejection, wrong-target-collection rejection, rollback case proving neither row exists after a later op fails.
increments.surface: Changed MCP tool: transact (new optional `ref` on create ops; '$ref:<name>' accepted in relation values and op ids; inputSchema + description updated).

increments.name: update_if op inside transact + dryRun plan
increments.what: (1) Extract updateEntryIfCore(dbc, ...) from updateEntryIf (same single-statement conditional UPDATE) and add op kind {op:'update_if', collection, id, if?, data?, increment?} to transact. A 0-row CAS is not a pg error, so the still-open transaction stays healthy: transact calls B1's diagnoseCasFailure with the TX executor — inside the transaction the diagnostic SELECT sees exactly the row version the UPDATE saw, so the diagnosis is race-free on this path (no hedging needed in the transact message) — and throws TxAbort with E_NOT_FOUND or E_CONFLICT plus the precise reason from B1's taxonomy (conflict/unset/bounds), e.g. 'op[1] update_if "seats": increment seats by -1 would go below min 0 — current value is 0; transaction rolled back — no ops applied'. This completes the book-a-seat composite: CAS seat decrement + booking create, atomically. (2) dryRun: true — run collection resolution, $ref graph checks, buildEntrySchema validation, and verifyRefs for every op, then return ok({applied:false, dryRun:true, plan:[{index, op, collection, valid:true}]}) without opening a transaction; on any failure it returns the same op-indexed error the real run would. Description documents that update_if conditions are still evaluated only at execute time (dryRun cannot pre-check a race). Extend smoke: seat-decrement + booking-create batch under a concurrent competing CAS (one wins, one gets E_CONFLICT with zero partial rows), absent-field increment inside transact gets the 'unset' message with op index, dryRun returns plan and writes nothing.
increments.surface: Changed MCP tool: transact (new op kind update_if; new top-level dryRun flag; inputSchema + description updated).

increments.name: Batch idempotency: transact idempotencyKey + receipts ledger
increments.what: (1) New table transact_receipts in db/schema.ts: id uuid, projectId FK cascade, idempotencyKey text, results jsonb ([{op, collection, id}]), createdAt; unique index (projectId, idempotencyKey) with a distinctive name (e.g. transact_receipts_key_idx) so conflict discrimination by constraint name stays unambiguous vs entries_uq_*/entries_idempotency_*. (2) transact accepts optional idempotencyKey (string, same shape as create_entry's). When present, the FIRST statement inside withTransaction INSERTs the receipt — the complete per-op result identity is already known pre-execution (create ids pre-generated in B2, other ids are inputs), so results are written in that same insert, no trailing UPDATE, no savepoints. A 23505 on transact_receipts_key_idx aborts the tx with nothing else executed: catch, roll back, SELECT the receipt on the neon-http client, and return ok({applied:true, replayed:true, results}) — mirroring create_entry's conflict-then-select replay (lib/entries.ts:168-179). Any other unique violation still funnels through rethrowUnique + TxAbort as in B2. Replay returns ids only (not data snapshots); the description says to re-query for current data. (3) Tool description updated: 'pass idempotencyKey to make retries safe — a replayed batch returns the original result ids with replayed:true'; the B2 blind-retry warning is narrowed to un-keyed batches. (4) Extend 17-transact.test.mjs: same key twice → second call returns replayed:true with identical ids and no duplicate rows/events; same key with a competing concurrent call (one commits, one replays); a batch whose ENTRY unique-field violation rolls back does NOT consume the key (receipt rolled back too — retry after fixing the data succeeds).
increments.surface: Changed MCP tool: transact (optional idempotencyKey; replayed:true in ok payload). New table transact_receipts (internal, no read surface yet).
increments.migration: new table transact_receipts via db/schema.ts + npm run db:push (repo has no migration files; drizzle-kit push is the workflow)

design.invariantCompliance: Rule 1 (declarative + self-describing): transact is pure data — an ops array in the same vocabulary agents already know (create/update/delete/update_if args are byte-identical to the single-op tools; `if` reuses WHERE_ITEM_JSON/whereItemSchema). The TOOL_DEF description states every boundary out loud: all-or-nothing; max 25 ops; entry ops only; $ref only in relation fields and op ids; events after commit only; MCP-only; the deliberate divergence 'unlike delete_entry, a delete op on a missing entry aborts the whole batch with E_NOT_FOUND'; and the retry semantics (blind-retry hazard for un-keyed batches until/unless idempotencyKey is passed, replayed:true on replay). Rule 2 (machine-readable errors): every failure carries an existing ErrorCode plus an op-indexed message that is itself the fix, and — critically — the CAS diagnosis is now guaranteed truthful: guard verdicts come from one Postgres-evaluated SELECT built from the same compiled fragments as the failed UPDATE, so NULL semantics cannot diverge; the decision tree is total (not_found / failing if-clause with actual value / unset increment field with 'set it first' hint / bounds with real current value / concurrent-change fallback), messages list all failed guards, single-op messages hedge 'as of the latest read', and inside transact the diagnosis runs on the open tx so it is exactly the row the UPDATE saw. dryRun gives the full validation verdict before any write. Rule 3 (secrets are references): nothing new touches secrets — transact ops carry only entry data; the receipts ledger stores op/collection/id triples, never payload secrets; post-commit emission goes through the existing connectorSecret/webhookSigningSecret server-side paths. Rule 4 (destructive = plan + confirm): transact cannot touch schema (the ops enum excludes it — define_collection/delete_collection keep their confirm gates untouched); entry deletes inside transact are strictly MORE conservative than delete_entry (missing id aborts everything rather than silently no-oping), and dryRun provides the Terraform-style plan for batches. Rule 5 (strict validation never weakens): every op flows through the same buildEntrySchema strict parse + verifyRefs + rethrowUnique — the cores are the SAME code the single-op paths call, parameterized by executor; $ref substitution happens before validation so the validator sees the real stored values; assumeExisting only skips a DB probe for ids the same transaction is guaranteed to insert first; unique fields remain DB-enforced (violations roll back the batch, and the idempotency-replay-vs-unique-violation distinction is preserved for batches via constraint-name discrimination, extending the existing data-core invariant rather than weakening it); all SQL — including the diagnostic SELECT's guard columns — is drizzle-parameterized fragments already meta-validated by buildWhere/field defs, adding no raw-SQL surface; per-field publicRead/delivery projection is untouched (transact is MCP-only).
crossGapDependencies: Events/change-feed gap: B1's pre-image makes CAS 'updated' payloads carry previous/changedFields, closing the documented change-feed hole for the CAS path (lib/entries.ts:316 gotcha) — that gap should build on this rather than re-deriving diffs.

Events/durable-queue gap: transact's post-commit emission list is handed to the single emit point (emitEntryEvent); if that gap swaps deliverWebhook's in-process retries for a pg-backed queue, transact inherits it with zero changes — no queue machinery is designed here.

Infra/Render + pg-job-queue gap: lib/db-tx.ts withTransaction is the interactive-transaction primitive that resolves the SYSTEM-REVIEW gotcha 'neon-http can't do FOR UPDATE SKIP LOCKED' — the queue gap can reuse it instead of building its own client.

Delivery/query gap: the buildWhereParts export B1 adds to lib/query.ts (per-clause compiled fragments) is also the natural building block if the delivery gap ever wants per-clause explain/validation surfaces; and the separately-flagged matchClause NULL-semantics fix (spawned task) keeps single-entry row gates consistent with the SQL semantics this design now treats as canonical.

No dependency on any time-flow/jobs-runner machinery: transact is synchronous within one request; retries and scheduling remain other gaps' concerns. transact_receipts retention (no TTL sweeper yet) is a natural first customer for a future jobs gap.
risks: WebSocket pool latency: per-call Pool setup adds ~100-300ms to each transact call (TLS + WS handshake to Neon). Acceptable for a batch primitive; do NOT cache the pool across invocations on serverless without deliberate lifecycle work.
Bundling `ws` in Next 15 may need serverExternalPackages: ['ws'] (pattern already exists at next.config.ts:4 for the MCP SDK); verify next build on Netlify before shipping B2.
TypeScript executor typing: NeonHttpDatabase vs NeonDatabase don't share a convenient common type for the cores — plan a minimal structural DbExecutor type; budget fiddling time in B2.

Single-op diagnosis is still a second statement on neon-http: between the failed UPDATE and the diagnostic SELECT the row can change, so a different guard may be blamed than the one that actually failed. Mitigated three ways: verdicts are internally consistent (one SELECT, one row version), messages hedge 'as of the latest read', and the all-guards-pass fallback names the concurrency explicitly. Inside transact (B4) the diagnosis is race-free.

buildWhereParts refactor touches the delivery query path's compile pipeline: buildWhere must keep byte-identical output (compose from the parts) or list gating regresses — the existing smoke suite plus a direct assertion in 14-update-if.test.mjs guards this.

transact_receipts grows unboundedly with keyed batches; no TTL sweeper exists (no cron by design). Bounded in practice by agent usage, but note a periodic cleanup as a follow-up for the jobs gap; createdAt is indexed-adjacent (projectId unique idx) so a manual purge is cheap.

Constraint-name discrimination for replay depends on the receipt index name staying stable and distinct from entries_uq_*/entries_idempotency_* prefixes — codify the name in one shared constant next to the rethrowUnique matcher so a rename cannot silently break replay detection.

Long transactions hold a row-lock window: up to 25 sequential ops over one WS connection keep locks for the whole batch; the 25-op cap and doing ALL validation/network work before the tx bound this, but batched CAS ops on hot rows add contention.

pg aborts a tx on any error: diagnostic reads after a UNIQUE VIOLATION must happen after rollback on the neon-http client, never inside the dead tx; only the 0-row-CAS diagnosis (not an error) and the insert-first receipt conflict (nothing else executed) are safe to handle in/around the tx — easy to get wrong in B4/B5, so smoke tests must cover the unique-violation-then-diagnose path.

B1 pre-image is advisory: under contention the event's `previous` can be one version behind the row the CAS actually matched; conditions themselves remain atomically checked in the UPDATE — document in the payload semantics.
Smoke-suite hygiene: an unclosed Pool keeps node --test alive; 17-transact.test.mjs must exercise pool.end() paths (suite runs --test-concurrency=1, so a hang blocks everything).

verifyRefs runs before the tx (unchanged trust model — relation values have no FK), so an entry deleted between validation and commit slips through exactly as it does for create_entry today; not a regression, but stated in the transact description.

openMinor.issue: B3 lets a batch write a dangling relation that today's write-time verifyRefs invariant would reject: because ALL validation/verifyRefs runs BEFORE the tx and assumeExisting skips the DB probe for pre-generated ids, a batch like [create order (ref:o), delete id:'$ref:o', create line_item {order:'$ref:o'}] (or delete after the referencing insert) commits a line_item whose relation points at a row deleted in the same transaction. The static checks described ('refs only point to earlier creates', 'targetCollection matches') do not catch delete ordering. Unlike the acknowledged pre-tx TOCTOU risk (concurrent deleter, unchanged trust model), this one is fully visible to static analysis of the ops array — the design just doesn't check it.
openMinor.suggestedFix: Add one more static check to the pre-validation pass: an id introduced by ref may not be the target of a delete op anywhere in the same batch if any op references it ('$ref:o is referenced by op[2] but deleted by op[1] — remove the delete or the reference'). Same for a concrete uuid that appears both as a delete target and as a relation value later in the batch. Add a smoke case asserting the rejection.

openMinor.issue: Contradictory delete semantics inside transact: the design says entry deletes 'match existing delete_entry semantics exactly', but existing deleteEntry is silently idempotent — a missing row is a no-op success (lib/entries.ts:333-337 only emits/audits `if (row)`, and the MCP handler returns ok({deleted:id}) regardless, lib/mcp/tools.ts:839-844) — while B2 specifies 'any ... 0-row update/delete throws TxAbort' with E_NOT_FOUND and full rollback. Both behaviors are defensible (abort is arguably right for all-or-nothing), but the spec claims equivalence it doesn't have; an agent that learned delete_entry's tolerance will get surprise rollbacks of otherwise-valid batches.
openMinor.suggestedFix: Pick abort-on-missing (correct for an atomic batch) and drop the 'match delete_entry exactly' claim; state the divergence out loud in the transact tool description per rule 1: 'unlike delete_entry, a delete op on a missing id aborts the whole batch (E_NOT_FOUND)'. Add a smoke assertion for it.

openMinor.issue: updateEntryCore keeps the read-merge-write pattern (SELECT current, shallow-merge, UPDATE — lib/entries.ts:193-211) without FOR UPDATE even when running inside the new interactive transaction. Under READ COMMITTED a concurrent committed write between the two statements is silently clobbered by the merge (lost update). This matches today's updateEntry, so it is not a regression — but inside a tool literally named transact, agents will assume stronger consistency, and the interactive tx makes the fix nearly free where it was impossible on neon-http.
openMinor.suggestedFix: In updateEntryCore (and the B4 update_if diagnostic re-read), issue the current-row fetch as SELECT ... FOR UPDATE when the executor is transactional — one flag on the core, no behavior change for the neon-http wrapper path. Document in the transact description that update ops lock their row for the duration of the batch (which also strengthens the 25-op lock-window note already in the risks).

openMinor.issue: rethrowUnique/dbErrorText (lib/entries.ts:114-126) parse the error message plus a top-level `.constraint` property, tuned to neon-http's NeonDbError shape. The neon-serverless WS driver surfaces node-postgres DatabaseError, and drizzle may wrap it differently — if the entries_uq_ pattern no longer appears in message+constraint, a unique violation inside transact degrades from an agent-repairable E_VALIDATION ('value already exists — this field is unique') to an opaque E_INTERNAL with a raw pg message, breaking rule 2 on the new path only. The planned B2 smoke test asserts 'unique-violation rollback' but not the error code/message shape.
openMinor.suggestedFix: In B2, extend dbErrorText to also walk `e.cause` (drizzle wraps driver errors as the cause in recent versions) and make the 17-transact.test.mjs unique-violation case assert the exact E_VALIDATION code and the 'value already exists — this field is unique' message with the op-index prefix, not just that zero rows exist after rollback.

openMinor.issue: B2 double-validates every op: the spec says transact runs 'zod shape, mustCollection, buildEntrySchema, verifyRefs' for all ops BEFORE the tx, and separately says the cores do 'the exact same buildEntrySchema-strict-parse + verifyRefs + rethrowUnique work' when invoked inside the tx. As written that is two strict-parses and two verifyRefs probe rounds per op — verifyRefs costs 1-2 HTTPS round-trips each (lib/entries.ts:29-97), so a 25-op batch wastes up to ~50 Neon round-trips and needlessly widens the row-lock window the risks section worries about (validation was supposed to happen before the tx opens).
openMinor.suggestedFix: One sentence in B2 deciding the layering: cores accept already-validated data (transact passes clean data + a validated:true contract), OR cores keep validation and transact's pre-pass only does the cheap static work (zod shape, collection resolution, $ref graph) while verifyRefs runs once inside the core. Either is fine; the spec must not say both.

openMinor.issue: B2 is borderline L, not M. It bundles: a new DB driver layer (lib/db-tx.ts + ws dep + next.config bundling verification on Netlify — itself flagged as a risk), a refactor of three mutators into descriptor-returning cores while claiming zero behavior change, the transact function with TxAbort plumbing, a new MCP tool def + switch case, and a 4-scenario smoke file with a webhook receiver. The design's own risks section pre-budgets 'fiddling time' for executor typing, which is a tell.
openMinor.suggestedFix: Split B2 into B2a (lib/db-tx.ts + core extraction + wrappers; zero-behavior-change refactor whose regression net is the existing 93-test smoke suite plus a trivial withTransaction round-trip test — independently shippable) and B2b (transact tool + TxAbort + smoke scenarios, depending on B2a). Each is a clean M/S.

openMinor.issue: The overlap between transact-with-creates and the existing bulk_create_entries (lib/entries.ts:386-438, ≤100 ops, per-item partial success, single multi-row insert) is never addressed. Two multi-create surfaces with opposite failure semantics (partial success vs all-or-nothing) and different caps (100 vs 25) will confuse agents choosing a tool; the map's own gotcha notes bulk's whole-batch unique-violation failure already blurs the line.
openMinor.suggestedFix: Cross-reference in both tool descriptions: transact says 'for bulk independent creates where partial success is acceptable, use bulk_create_entries (higher cap, per-item results)'; bulk_create_entries gains the mirror sentence pointing at transact for all-or-nothing. One line each in B2's tool-description work item.

openMinor.issue: updateEntryCore inside the transaction keeps updateEntry's unlocked read-modify-write merge (SELECT current, merge, UPDATE — lib/entries.ts:193-211). Under READ COMMITTED a concurrent writer between the in-tx SELECT and UPDATE is silently lost. Not a regression versus today's updateEntry, but a tool named 'transact' invites agents to assume isolation it doesn't provide — and the design conspicuously has the fix available for free: the whole point of the WS driver is that FOR UPDATE now works.
openMinor.suggestedFix: Have updateEntryCore's current-row fetch use SELECT ... FOR UPDATE when running under withTransaction (executor flag or separate query), and/or state the isolation boundary in the transact description ('plain update ops are last-write-wins under concurrency; use update_if for guarded writes'). Cheap either way; pick one in B2/B4.
